coredata.dc:title: A high-performance composite CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower photocatalyst: Synergy of complex-sensitization, TiO2-morphology control and carbon dot-surface modification
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yunning
Yang, Lu
Sun, Yingnan
Guan, Renquan
Liu, Di
Zhao, Jie
Shang, Qingkun
coredata.dc:description: A CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite photocatalyst (CDs: carbon dots, HQCA: 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-carboxylic acid) was constructed by exploiting the synergy of complex-sensitization, TiO2-morphology control, and carbon dot-surface modification. The photocatalytic degradation of phenol, norfloxacin, tetrabromobisphenol A and Cr (VI) by Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particle, tube, diamond and flower-like show that the Cu-HQCA complex effectively extends the visible light absorption range of composite photocatalysts, and the flower morphology of TiO2 not only improves the adsorption of pollutants but also enhances the photoelectric response of the composite photocatalyst. In this manner, a CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst that demonstrates excellent performance for the removal of dexamethasone, which does not degrade well naturally, was prepared, which delivered a rate of degradation of 99.4% and a degree of mineralization of 77.4% for dexamethasone. Possible degradation sites and dexamethasone degradation products were studied by combining computational chemistry with high-resolution mass spectrometry, and a possible mechanism for photogenerated carrier transmission and separation in the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers is proposed.

head.ce:title: A high-performance composite CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower photocatalyst: Synergy of complex-sensitization, TiO2-morphology control and carbon dot-surface modification

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite photocatalyst (CDs: carbon dots, HQCA: 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-carboxylic acid) was constructed by exploiting the synergy of complex-sensitization, TiO2-morphology control, and carbon dot-surface modification. The photocatalytic degradation of phenol, norfloxacin, tetrabromobisphenol A and Cr (VI) by Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particle, tube, diamond and flower-like show that the Cu-HQCA complex effectively extends the visible light absorption range of composite photocatalysts, and the flower morphology of TiO2 not only improves the adsorption of pollutants but also enhances the photoelectric response of the composite photocatalyst. In this manner, a CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst that demonstrates excellent performance for the removal of dexamethasone, which does not degrade well naturally, was prepared, which delivered a rate of degradation of 99.4% and a degree of mineralization of 77.4% for dexamethasone. Possible degradation sites and dexamethasone degradation products were studied by combining computational chemistry with high-resolution mass spectrometry, and a possible mechanism for photogenerated carrier transmission and separation in the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers is proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: As society’s demands for medical and health protection continues to increase, the amount of medical wastewater discharged into rivers and lakes is expected to inevitably increase, leading to serious water pollution and the endangerment of water ecosystems [1]. For example, dexamethasone, a commonly used and effective glucocorticoid, has been detected in medical wastewater and rivers [2–3], and poses a significant threat to the growth and reproduction of aquatic organisms and the terrestrial environment, even at very low levels [4]. Dexamethasone can enter the human body via ecological water, and excessive levels in the body can cause severe osteoporosis, endocrine disorders, mental disorders, digestive complications, and fetal malformations.

Various methods for removing pharmaceutical contaminants from wastewater have emerged over the past few decades, including membrane bioreactors and biodegradation [5–8], anaerobic reactors [9], electrical coagulation processes [10,11], adsorption processes [12], and advanced oxidation technologies [2,13–15]. However, these methods are not very effective against glucocorticoids. On the other hand, photocatalyst technology is widely used to reduce CO2 and degrade organic pollutants [16–23], which is ascribable to its environmental friendliness and safety. Some researchers have recently explored the use of photocatalyst technology to degrade pharmaceutical pollutants, such as dexamethasone, with Ag/TiO2, WO3, and ZrO2 used under ultraviolet or visible light, or even high-energy gamma radiation [24–26]. These studies revealed that rapidly degrading and fully mineralizing dexamethasone is extremely difficult.

Since its discovery in 1972 by Fujishima TiO2 has been widely used as a photocatalyst [27–30]. However, low visible light utilization and fast carrier recombination still restrict the development of TiO2. To overcome these disadvantages, many new TiO2-based composite photocatalysts have been synthesized through elemental doping [31–35], surface sensitization and modification [36–45], morphology control [46–52], and semiconductor combination [53–56]. However, achieving high- degradation and mineralization efficiencies for dexamethasone using a single strategy for improving the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 is difficult. Instead, such strategies should simultaneously improve light-absorption and utilization efficiency, accelerating the separation and transmission of photogenerated electrons and holes, and effectively inhibiting their recombination [57–64]. In other words, multiple methods should be used simultaneously, including surface sensitization and morphology control [65].

In this study, we constructed a CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite photocatalyst by combining complex-surface sensitization, TiO2 -morphology control, and carbon dot-surface modification to deliver highly efficient dexamethasone degradation and mineralization Cu-HQCA, a simple small metal–organic molecular complex effectively improves the visible light absorption and utilization efficiency of TiO2. Among various TiO2 morphologies, including particles, nanotubes, diamonds, and flowers, the latter not only facilitates photogenerated carrier transmission, but also enhances the adsorption of pollutants on its surfaces. CDs are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with diameters of less than 10 nm. The large graphene-like conjugated planes of CDs greatly accelerate the separation and transmission of photogenerated carriers. The CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of dexamethasone, with a rate of degradation of 99.4% and a degree of mineralization of 77.4% determined. Additionally, the process involved in the degradation of dexamethasone and the products formed have been explored in detail by combined high-resolution mass spectrometry and computational chemistry. The main degradation pathway and a possible degradation mechanism are also proposed. We exploited the synergy between complex sensitization, morphology control, and surface modification to effectively improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2, while not increase the difficulty and cost involved in preparing the composite photocatalyst. The systematic research work in this article provides a typical example of the construction of an efficient composite photocatalyst capable of removing pollutants that are extremely difficult to degrade.

All reagents and solvents were used directly and without further purification. Tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT) and copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O) as well as dexamethasone (DXMS) were purchased from Macklin company (Shanghai, China). 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-carboxylic acid (HQCA) was purchased from Chinese Academy of Sciences - Yanshen Technology limited company (Changchun, China). Potassium chloride (KCl), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), isopropanol, sodium ethylenediamine tetra acetate (EDTA-2Na), urea, absolute ethyl alcohol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Phenol, TetrabromobisphenolA (TBBPA), norfloxacin (NFX), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), P-benzoquinone, p-phenylenediamine and formic acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Luminescent bacteria (LB) were purchased from Haifuda Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Chromatographic pure methanol and acetonitrile were purchased from TEDIA (American). Methanol and acetonitrile (for LC/MS) were purchased from Fisher Chemical (USA).

Cu-HQCA was synthesized by simple reaction of copper salt and organic ligand with the ratio 1:2. Briefly, amount of 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-carboxylic acid (HQCA) was added into dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) under vigorous stirring in the 60 ℃ water bath. Then, a certain of copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O) was added into the solution and react for 4 h to obtain the Cu-HQCA solution (in DMSO).

TiO2 particle and flower were prepared by one step hydrothermal methods which reported in our previous work [66]. TiO2 tube was synthesized by hydrothermal methods. Briefly, 0.6 g TiO2 particle were dispersed into 60 mL sodium hydroxide solution (10 mol/L) under stirring. After 30 min, the mixture was transferred to the 100 mL teflon autoclave and react for 48 h at 150℃. After cooling to room temperature the suspension was separated by centrifugation and washed with 0.1 mol/L hydrochloric acid aqueous solution and deionized water until the pH equal to 7, then the solid was dried at 80℃. Finally, the obtained solid was calcined in a muffle furnace for 2 h at 400℃.

Diamond-like TiO2 was prepared by two steps. (1) Synthesis of precursors: 2.5 mL TBOT was dispersed into 250 mL beaker which contains 100 mL anhydrous ethanol and amount of KCl (0.1 mol/L) under vigorousstirring. After 10 min, the mixture was aged for 24 h at room temperature. Then the suspensions were separated by centrifugation and rinsed with deionized water and absolute alcohol for three times, respectively. Finally, the solid precursors were dried at 80℃. (2) Synthesis of diamond-like TiO2: 0.5 g precursor was dispersed into 70 mL 0.1 mol/L NH4F water solution under vigorous stirring for 10 min. Then the solution was transferred to the 100 mL teflon autoclave and react for 12 h at 180 ℃. Cooling to room temperature, the suspension was separated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water and absolute alcohol for three times, respectively. Finally, the solid was dried at 80 ℃.

A certain amount of TiO2 (particle, or tube, or diamond, or flower) were dispersed into the 30 mL Cu-HQCA DMSO solution respectively. It was vigorous stirring for 30 min. Then, the reaction solution was transferred to the 50 mL Teflon autoclave and maintained for 8 h at 100 ℃. The solid products were centrifuged and washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol for three times, respectively. The final solid was dried at 80℃. Finally, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particle, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 tube, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 diamond, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower were obtained, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the polychromatic carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous literature [67], polychromatic CDs was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.1 g of P-phenylenediamine and 0.1 g of urea were added to 30 mL of water and stirring for 30 min. Then, the water solution was transferred to the 50 mL Teflon autoclave and reacted at 160℃ for 10 h. The result solution was filtered with 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the obtained pure solution was freeze-dried in vacuum and the purple CDs solid were obtained.

As shown in Scheme 1 , 0.15 g Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower was dispersed into the 250 mL round bottom flask which contains 100 mL CDs water solution, and the mixed solution was heated at 60 ℃ and refluxed for 12 h. After reaction was completed, the suspension was centrifuged and the obtained solid was washed with water and anhydrous ethanol for three times, respectively. Then the solid was dried at 80 ℃.

Replace Cu-HQCA/TiO2 with TiO2, CDs/TiO2 flower was prepared under the same conditions. On this basis, according to the method in section 2.2.3, Cu-HQCA@CDs/TiO2 flower was obtained by replacing TiO2 with CDs/TiO2.

The morphology and element analysis of the photocatalysts were characterized with Filed-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI, Japan) and Field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 PLUS, Japan) (Matrix: T200-Mo). The crystal structure and phase composition of photocatalysts was measured by X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Smartlab, Japan) with Cukα radiation (kα was equal to 0.15406 nm). The optical properties of photocatalysts were tested by UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Cary 7000, Agilent, USA). The surface properties (specific surface area and pore size distribution) of photocatalysts were measured by gas adsorption detector (ASAP 2020 M, Microtrac, USA). Elemental composition, valence state and the electronic power of photocatalysts were measured by Multifunctional X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB250, USA). Photocurrent density and impedance of photocatalysts were tested with three electrode system by Electrochemical workstation (CHI 660C, CH, China). The steady-state fluorescence spectra and transient fluorescence spectra of photocatalysts were determined by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-320, China) and full function fluorescence spectrum system (FLS920P, Edinburgh, England), respectively. Total organic carbon content was determined by Total organic carbon analyzer (TOC, Elementer Vario TOC, Germany). The concentration of phenol, TBBPA and NFX before and after photocatalysis were measured by High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1200, USA). The concentration of Cr (VI) was tested by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Genesys 10S, Thermol Scientific, USA). The concentration of dexamethasone and the degradation products of dexamethasone were measured by Quadrupole electrostatic field orbital trap high resolution liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (Q Exactive Focus, Thermo, USA). The signals of superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals produced by the photocatalyst were determined by Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (A300-10/12, Bruker). Infrared spectra of photocatalysts were tested by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer FT-IR (Thermal, Nicolet 6700, America). Luminescent intensity of luminescent bacteria was obtained by Microplate Reader (Infinite M Plex, Austria).

50 mg photocatalysts were added into the phenol, or norfloxacin, or K2Cr2O7, or tetrabromobisphenol A and dexamethasone water solution (50 mL, 10 mg/L) and the system was stirred for 30 min in dark environment to reach the balance of absorption–desorption. (Note: Since tetrabromobisphenol A is almost insoluble in water, in the experiment acetonitrile was used as the solvent to first prepare a mother liquor with a concentration of 500 mg/L, and then acetonitrile: water (volume ratio of 1:1) was used to prepare a tetrabromobisphenol working solution with a concentration of 10 ppm). After that, the system was carried out under the visible light (λ＞420 nm) by Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, Beijing CeAulight) equipped with 420 nm filter. In this process, 0.1 mL reaction solution was taken out at a certain times interval and filtered with 0.22 μm membrane. Then the concentration of phenol, TBBPA and NFX was analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography and the concentration of Cr (VI) was tested by ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer. And the concentration of dexamethasone after degradation was analyzed by Quadrupole electrostatic field orbital trap high resolution liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS-MS) The concentration of dexamethasone was calculated by the following formula:

where: y is peak area; x is the concentration of dexamethasone.

The mineralization degree of phenol, norfloxacin and dexamethasone was detected by total organic carbon analyzer. The mineralization rate of pollutant was calculated by the following equation:

Where TOC0 was the total organic carbon value of original pollutant such as phenol, norfloxacin and dexamethasone; TOCt was the total organic carbon value of pollutant after degraded by photocatalysts at certain time t.

In addition, the dexamethasone degradation system was also carried out under different light with single wavelength (Purple light λ = 420 nm; Blue light λ = 460 nm, Green light λ = 550 nm, Yellow light λ = 578 nm, Orange red light λ = 600 nm and Red light λ = 630 nm).

In order to investigate the photocatalysts cycle stability, we used the TiO2 and TiO2 composites photocatalysts to degrade organic pollutants for 5 cycles. First, in the first cycle, five parallel photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out with fresh TiO2 or TiO2 composites photocatalysts. After the photocatalytic degradation were completed, the photocatalyst was recovered and applied to the secondary photocatalysis. Then the photocatalyst recovered in the second cycle was used in the third photocatalysis. Finally, continue to the 5th time.

Chemical method was carried out to determine the main active species in the process of photocatalysis. Use isopropanol, p-benzoquinone and EDTA·2Na as capture agent to capture hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals and holes, respectively. Carry out the photocatalysis experiments according to the section 2.4.1, and add a certain amount of capture agent before starting the light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Liquid phase and high resolution mass spectrometry test conditions
ce:section.ce:para: HPLC: C-18 Column, Diode array detector, Detection wavelength (210 nm for phenol and TBBPA; 278 nm for NFX), Mobile phase ratio (water: methanol 50:50 for phenol; water:acetonitrile 40:60 for TBBPA; 0.1% Formic acid: methanol: acetonitrile 70:15:15 for NFX).

HPLC-MS-MS: Positive ion mode, m/z range is 50–450, Electrospray ion source and the working condition: Sheath gas flower rate is 30; Aus gas flow rate is15; Sweep gas flow rate is 0; Spray voltage is 4.2; Capillary temperature is 320 ℃ and Aus gas heater temperature is 300℃.

First, take out Freeze-dried luminescent bacteria from the freezer and equilibrate at room temperature for 10 min. Second, use 500 μL of resuscitation solution to resurrect photobacterium luminescens, and then equilibrate at room temperature for 15 min. Third, 900 μL 10 mg/L phenol, or norfloxacin, or tetrabromobisphenol A or Cr(VI), or dexamethasone or it’s degradation products was mixed with 100 μL osmotic pressure regulating fluid, and 50 μL of bright luminescent bacterial was added. The luminous intensity was tested after 15 min reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of TiO2 morphology on photocatalytic performance of Cu-HQCA/TiO2

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 shows TEM and HRTEM images of four morphologically different pure TiO2 photocatalysts, with particle, tube, diamond, and flower morphologies clearly visible (Fig. S1(a1-d1)); the TiO2 particles, tubes, and flowers are approximately 20 nm, 10 nm, and 2 μm in size, respectively, while the TiO2 diamond has dimensions of 120 × 80 nm. All four photocatalysts exhibit clear 0.35 nm lattice stripes that correspond to the {101} crystal face of TiO2 (Fig. S1(a2-d2)). The XRD patterns of the four TiO2 photocatalysts (Fig. S2 (a)) exhibit seven diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 25.41°, 37.56°, 48.04°, 54.14°, 55.03°, 62.52°, and 75.11° that correspond to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (215) crystal planes of anatase TiO2 (referenced against JCPDS standard card 21–1272), respectively, which reveals that all four samples are anatase TiO2 despite their different morphologies. The UV–vis spectra of the four pure TiO2 samples and P25 are displayed in Fig. S2(b); clearly, each sample only absorbs ultraviolet light in the 200–400 nm range. BET analyses (Fig. S2(c)) reveal specific surface areas of 67.12 m2/g, 145.25 m2/g, 153.28 m2/g, and 172.27 m2/g for the TiO2 particles, diamonds, tubes, and flowers, respectively.

Fig. S2(d) shows the phenol-degradation efficiencies of the four TiO2 samples and P25 when irradiated with visible light. The TiO2 particles, diamonds, tubes, flowers, and P25 photocatalysts had adsorbed approximately 0.10%, 0.91%, 0.31%, 1.36%, and 1.10% phenol after stirring for 30 min in the dark. Their phenol-degradation performance under visible light is not ideal; the TiO2 flowers exhibited the highest phenol-degradation efficiency (3.76%), followed by the diamonds (3.05%), P25 (1.84%), the tubes (1.66%), the TiO2 particle (0.83%), which indicates that TiO2 morphology impacts both phenol adsorption and its photocatalytic degradation, albeit not very significantly. To improve the visible light absorbing ability of pure TiO2 and its capacity to degrade organic pollutants, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composite photocatalysts were prepared, in which Cu-HQCA, a complex with a conjugated plane, sensitizes TiO2.

TEM and HRTEM images, and energy spectra of the four above-mentioned Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composite photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 1 , which clearly shows particle, tube, diamond, and flower morphologies (Fig. 1(a1-d1)). The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composite photocatalysts are all composed of anatase TiO2; all four photocatalysts exhibit clear 0.35-nm lattice stripes that correspond to the {101} crystal face of anatase TiO2 (Fig. 1(a2-d2)). These data indicate that including the Cu-HQCA complex does not change the TiO2 morphology or lattice structure. The EDX spectra (Fig. 1(a3-d3)) show that the four photocatalysts contain Ti, O, C, N, and Cu, which indirectly confirms that the Cu-HQCA complex had been successfully combined with TiO2. Furthermore, XPS (Fig. S3) revealed that these four composite photocatalysts have the same chemical compositions, with Cu and N detected due to the presence of the Cu-HQCA complex. The EDS and XPS Cu signals are very weak because the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 photocatalysts contain only 2% Cu-HQCA. The four photocatalysts was subjected to ICP-MS to further confirm the presence of Cu2+, which was determined to be present at 2870.81 μg/mL, 2864.51 μg/mL, 2878.43 μg/mL, and 2885.11 μg/mL in the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particle, tube, diamond, and flower catalysts, respectively, consistent with the theoretical value of 2926 μg/mL.

Fig. S4(a) shows XRD spectra of the four Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composite photocatalysts, which displays seven diffraction peaks that correspond to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (215) crystal planes of anatase TiO2, similar to pure TiO2 (JCPDS 21–1272), which indicates that including the Cu-HQCA complex does not change the lattice structure of TiO2. The four composite photocatalysts strongly absorb in the 400–500 nm range (Fig. S4(b-c)), with each bandgap are calculated to be 3.20 eV according to the formula:
( α h ν )
2
= A
( h ν - E g )
. These results show that Cu-HQCA sensitization effectively expands the ability of TiO2 to absorb visible light without changing the intrinsic TiO2 absorption.

The surface properties of the four photocatalysts are different, which is ascribable their very different morphologies. The specific surface areas and pore size distributions of the four composite photocatalysts were investigated, the results of which are shown in Fig. S4(d-e). According to IUPAC definitions, the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 tubes, diamonds, and flowers exhibit type-IV adsorption /desorption isotherms, while those of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particles are type-II. Therefore, with the exception of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particles, all composite photocatalysts have mesoporous structures, with pore-size distribution ranges determined to be 20–50 nm(tubes), 10–30 nm(diamonds), and 10–20 nm (flowers). The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 diamonds and tubes show microporous (>50 nm) as well as mesoporous structures on their surfaces. The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers have the largest specific surface area (161.78 m2/g), followed by the tubes (146.33 m2/g), and diamonds (137.12 m2/g), with areas that are 2.9–2.6-, and 2.5 times larger than that of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particles, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic performance of Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, phenol (a representative phenolic), norfloxacin (NFX, an antibiotic), tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA, an environmental hormone), and K2Cr2O4 (a Cr(Ⅵ), heavy metal) were used as probes for evaluating the photocatalytic performance of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composite, the results of which are shown in Fig. S5. Fig. S5(a1-d1) reveals that the four Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composites show excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of the four abovementioned pollutants, with the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite exhibiting the best photocatalytic performance. Phenol, NFX, and TBBPA were degraded by approximately 99.6%, 97.1%, and 99%, while 96.5% of the Cr (VI) was reduced in 30 min by the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite. Moreover, these values increased to 100%, 96.7%, 100%, and 99.2%, respectively, when the degradation time was increased to 60 min. Fig. S5(a2-d2) shows degradation/reduction kinetic traces for phenol, NFX, TBBPA, and Cr(VI) using the four composite photocatalysts. The rate constants for the degradation/reduction reactions of the four pollutants are clearly different despite almost identical degradation efficiencies. The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite exhibited the largest rate constants for degradation/reduction of the four pollutants, which were determined to be 4.2-, 1.7-, 7.6-, and 2.1-times larger than those of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particle composite, respectively, 1.08-, 1.36-, 2.11-, and 1.25-times larger than those of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 tube composite, and 1.8-, 1.51-, 2.25-, and 1.13-times larger than those Cu-HQCA/TiO2 diamond composite. Moreover, the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite also exhibited the best cycling stability (Fig. S5(a3-d3), with phenol, NFX, TBBA, and Cr(VI) degraded/reduced by approximately 99%, 94.5%, 98%, and 95%, respectively, using the Cu-HQAC/TiO2 flower catalyst in the third cycle. In contrast, the Cu-HQAC/TiO2 particles exhibited values of 83%, 82%, 80%, and 70%, respectively, in the third cycle.

Adsorption is well known to be the premise of photocatalysis; better pollutant adsorption on the catalyst surface leads to more thorough photocatalytic degradation. Owing to their different surface characteristics, the four composite photocatalysts show very different adsorption performance toward the abovementioned four pollutants, which is possibly the main reason for the observed differences in photocatalytic behavior. The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite exhibited excellent adsorption performance for phenol, NFX, TBBPA, and Cr(VI), with approximately 3.1%, 50%, 1.1%, and 85.2% of these pollutants adsorbed, respectively. These data are closely related to the special three-dimensional flower-like structure and large specific surface area of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite.

The abilities of the four composite photocatalysts to mineralize phenol, NFX, and TBBPA were investigated for different illumination times, the results of which are shown in Fig. S6. The salinities of phenol, NFX, and TBBPA increased gradually with increasing degradation time. The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particle catalyst mineralized phenol much less than the other three photocatalysts. On the other hand, the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst mineralized NFX and TBBPA to a much greater extent than the other three photocatalysts, with maximum mineralization of 90.1%, 72.3%, and 60.4% determined for phenol, NFX, and TBBPA, respectively, while NFX and TBBPA were only decomposed by about 30% with the other three catalysts. Such low degrees of salinity indicate the presence of many degradation intermediates in these systems; hence, evaluating the environmental toxicities of these degradation intermediates is very important. Toxicities were evaluated by examining the luminescence intensities of photobacteria before and after reactions photocatalyzed by our Cu-HQCA/TiO2-based catalysts. Organic pollutants can affect bacterial metabolism, which weakens the luminescence intensities of these bacteria; a less-toxic pollutant results in only a slightly weakened intensity. Conversely, a more-toxic pollutant can significantly weaken or extinguish luminescence. Fig. S7 shows that all four pollutants decrease photobacterial luminance intensity, highlighting their environmental toxicities. However, with the exception of TBBPA, the fluorescence intensities of systems containing phenol, NFX, or Cr(VI) degradation/reduction products were almost identical to that of the blank sample or only slightly lower when formed using the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst under visible light for 1 h, which indicates that the environmental toxicities of phenol, NFX, and Cr(VI) are lower following photocatalytic degradation/reduction. TBBPA is somewhat environmentally toxic. Despite lower toxicity following photocatalytic degradation, this system presented the highest toxic among those examined.

Actual wastewater is usually a mixture of various organic and inorganic pollutants. Therefore, the universality of a photocatalyst is better reflected by the degradation/reduction of pollutant mixtures. Here, we examined the degradation of a mixture of phenol, NFX, and Cr (VI) by Cu-HQCA/TiO2 (but not TBBPA, which is insoluble in water). The concentration of each pollutant was set to 10 mg/ L, with 50 mg of the photocatalyst used. Fig. 2 reveals that all four photocatalysts rapidly degrade this pollutant mixture. The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite the best performance, with phenol, NFX, and Cr (VI) degraded/reduced by 95.7%, 97.6%, and 96.5%, respectively, within 30 min when irradiated with visible light. While the other three catalysts exhibited degradation rates for phenol, NFX, and Cr (VI) above 90%, they were not as efficient as the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower composite. Interestingly, maximum salinities of 76.7%, 49%, 66.7%, and 68.2% were observed, respectively, when using the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particle, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 tube and Cu-HQCA/TiO2 diamond catalysts, respectively. In addition, the environmental toxicities of the degradation products in the mixed system (Fig. S7) were also significantly lower. Fig. 2 also clearly shows that each pollutant exhibits very different adsorption characteristics on the surfaces of the four composite photocatalysts. In particular, approximately 85% of the Cr(VI) and 51% of the NFX were adsorb onto the surfaces of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, which one of the reasons for it exhibiting the highest photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectric characteristics of Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composite photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The photoelectric characteristics of the four Cu-HQCA/TiO2 composite photocatalysts were examined to better understand their different photocatalytic activities. The absorption spectra and bandgaps of the four composite photocatalysts the same because have the same confirmed in Fig. S4(b, c). However, the fluorescence spectra, photocurrent densities, electrochemical impedance spectra, and ESR spectra of these catalysts (Fig. S8) are quite different. The fluorescence spectra of the four composite photocatalysts are similar when excited at the same wavelength, but the intensity of the fluorescence-emission peak was observed to decreased in the order: Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particles > tubes > diamonds > flowers. Moreover, the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst exhibited the longest fluorescence lifetime (Table S1). Meanwhile, the photocurrent density was oppositely ordered; the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst exhibited the largest photocurrent density twice that of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 tubes or diamonds and 2.6 times that of the of Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particles. The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 particle catalyst showed the largest impedance radius, while that of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst was the smallest. Lower fluorescence intensities are known to correspond to longer fluorescence lifetimes, higher photocurrent densities, and smaller impedance rings, which results in higher photocarrier hole and electron separation efficiencies. In this way, the unique morphology and surface characteristics the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers create favorable conditions for the transmission and separation of photogenerated carriers, resulting in excellent photocatalytic performance. In addition, the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst produced the highest number of hydroxyl radicals •OH and superoxide radical •O2 −; hence, the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers decompose phenol, NFX, and TBBPA more efficiently and thoroughly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs surface modification on photocatalytic performance of Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower
ce:section.ce:para: The above studies show that morphology significantly influences the ability of TiO2 to photocatalytically degrade organic and inorganic pollutants. We next turned our attention to dexamethasone, which has played a role in treating COVID-19 but decomposes with difficult naturally. Only 75.6% of the dexamethasone was degraded by the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst within 4 h when illuminated by visible light. Studies have shown that the main metabolites of dexamethasone in the liver are 6-hydroxydexamethasone, 6-hydroxy-9-αF-A [68], which are not ring-opened products of dexamethasone; hence, they are still harmful to environmental water after being discharged from the body. To further increase dexamethasone degradation efficiency and mineralization degree, we further modified the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers with CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The micromorphologies of the CDs and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers were characterized by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. Fig. 3 compares the characteristics of the TiO2 flowers and the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers. The SEM images show that the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 surface appears to be covered with a layer of material. The TEM image in the inset also shows that the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower surface is covered. The HRTEM image of CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 clearly shows the 0.21 nm lattice spacing of associated with the CDs Fig. S9(a-c) shows the surface elemental distributions of the three flower-like TiO2 photocatalysts; Ti, O, C, N, and Cu are distributed on the surfaces of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, while only Ti and O are distributed on the surfaces of the TiO2 flowers which indicates that Cu-HQCA complexes and CDs had been successfully grafted onto the TiO2 flowers. Further BET analyses (Fig. S10) revealed that the specific surface area, average pore volume, and pore diameter of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers (132.28 m2/g, 0.34 cm3/g, and 8.53 nm) are all smaller than those of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers (161.78 m2/g, 0.49 cm3/g and 9.22 nm), which is ascribable to the CDs coating.

In order to understand how the CDs affect the photocatalytic performance of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, we characterized the interactions between the Cu-HQCA complexes and the TiO2 flowers, and between the CDs and Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers. UV–vis absorption, IR, and XPS spectra of the Cu-HQCA complex and Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers are shown in Fig. S11. The absorption spectra in Fig. S11(a) show that the absorption peak observed between 380 and 450 nm for the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers is derived from a metal–ligand electronic transition of the Cu-HQCA complex. The IR spectra of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers (Fig. S11(b)) show that the intensity of the carbonyl vibration absorption peak at 1683 cm−1 derived from the Cu-HQCA complex is weaker and the hydroxyl band centered at 3423 cm−1 is stronger than those observed for the Cu-HQCA complex. Hence, Cu-HQCA complexes coordinates to Ti3+ at the edges of the titanium dioxide lattice through their carboxyl groups, while also forming hydrogen bonds to water molecules adsorbed on the surfaces of the TiO2 flowers. Fig. S11(c) shows that the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst not only exhibits peaks corresponding to lattice oxygens derived from TiO2 (529.67 eV), but also hydroxyl (531.76 eV) and carbonyl (533.33 eV) oxygens derived from Cu-HQCA complexes (532.01 eV and 533.52 eV). Taken together, these results indicate that the Cu-HQCA complexes use carboxyl groups that are not involved in the coordination to interact with the TiO2 flowers.

The XRD, UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra, and XPS spectra of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower are presented in Fig. 4 . For comparative convenience, we also show relevant spectra for TiO2 and the CDs. The XRD spectra (Fig. 4(a)) show that the Cu-HQCA complexes and CDs do not change the anatase crystal-phase characteristics of TiO2. When compared, the UV diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of the CDs, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers (Fig. 4(b)) reveal that the excellent visible-light absorbing characteristics of the CDs (200–800 nm) do not alter the intrinsic absorption of TiO2, rather they greatly improve absorption performance above 400 nm. The Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers both exhibited bandgaps of 3.20 eV, as calculated from the formula: (αhν)2 = A(hν-Eg), indicating that Cu-HQCA complex inclusion and CDs modification did not change the intrinsic absorption and bandgap of the TiO2 flowers. Fig. 4(c) shows IR spectra of the CDs, TiO2 flowers, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers. Because the CDs bear nitrogen-containing unsaturated compounds, they still show characteristic IR absorptions for O–H, N–H, and unsaturated C–H moieties at above 3000 cm−1. These CDs spectral characteristics are clearly visible in the same region for the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers. CDs vibrational absorptions below 1000 cm−1 are also observed in the same area of the spectrum of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers and are slightly stronger than those observed for the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers. The O1s, C1s, and N1s XPS spectra of the TiO2 flowers, Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, CDs, and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers are presented in Fig. 4(d-f) (with the others presented in Fig. S12). The O1s spectra reveal the presence of both Ti-O-Ti lattice oxygens (529.74 eV; from TiO2) and O–H hydroxyl oxygens (531.89 eV) on the TiO2 surfaces of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers; the CDs enhance the strength of the hydroxyl oxygen peak compared to that of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers. The presence of the CDs resulted in a redshift in the bond energy of the C = O carbonyl oxygens in the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, from 533.33 to 533.53 eV, which is close to that of Cu-HQCA (Fig. S11(c)). The C1s spectrum of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers clearly show a C-O or C-N peak at 286.55 eV that is ascribable to the CDs. The N1s spectrum of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2flowers only shows a pyridinic nitrogen peak (399.40 eV), while the peak corresponding to graphitized nitrogen (402.52 eV) derived from the CDs is also present in the spectrum of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers. Based how the CDs influence the absorption, infrared, and XPS spectra of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, we conclude that the CDs directly modify the TiO2 surface with their active groups or interact with the Cu-HQCA complexes bound to the TiO2 surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower to the dexamethasone
ce:section.ce:para: Dexamethasone degradation efficiency, kinetics, and cycling stability were next investigated, the results of which are shown in Fig. 5 . CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers exhibit excellent degradation performance, 99.4% of the dexamethasone degraded in 240 min, which is 1.31–1.46-, and 7.6-times higher than those of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower, TiO2 flower, and P25 catalysts respectively. The rate constant for the degradation of dexamethasone by the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst was determined to be 0.4014h-1, which is higher than those of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers (0.2309 h−1), TiO2 flower (0.2875 h−1), and P25 (0.02370 h−1). The CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers also showed high photocatalytic cycling stability, with approximately 91.2% of the dexamethasone degraded in the fifth cycle. We confirmed that the degradation efficiency, rate, and cycling stability of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst toward dexamethasone are greatly improved compared to those of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers (fresh and five-times used) were acquired to confirm the stability of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers, and are shown in Fig. S13. The absorption intensities of the five-times-used composite photocatalysts are slightly lower than those of the fresh catalysts; however, the positions of their absorption peaks hardly changed, which indicates that both the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers and the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers have superior stabilities.

The CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers exhibited a dexamethasone mineralization degree of 77.4%, which is 1.67- and 1.73-times higher than those of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers (46.4%) and TiO2 flowers (44.6%). In addition, dexamethasone was mineralized by about 13.8%, 32.4%, and 58.5% in the presence of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst when irradiated with visible light for 1, 2, and 3 h, respectively.

Carrier separation efficiency is well-known to play an important role in determining photocatalyst performance. Fig. 6 (a-d) displays transient fluorescence spectra, photocurrent densities, impedances, and surface photovoltages of the three flower-like photocatalysts. The CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower photocatalyst exhibited the lowest fluorescence intensity, the highest photocurrent density, the smallest impedance radius, and the highest surface photovoltage; it also showed the longest fluorescence lifetime (Table S2). These results reveal that the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst has the best carrier separation efficiency, although the CDs influence this slightly.

Active species play important roles in the entire photocatalysis process. Fig. 6(e and f) show ESR spectra acquired under visible light. While the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower and Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalysts appear to generate similar levels of •O2 − based on their almost equal signal intensities, the former appears to generate significantly more •OH. Pure TiO2 flowers produced significantly stronger •O2 − and •OH signals after being modified with carbon dots; hence the number of active groups appears to be the fundamental reason for the superior photocatalytic activity.

We also investigated the degradation efficiency and kinetics of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst during the degradation of dexamethasone when irradiated with light of various wavelengths, with the results shown in Fig. S14. When the absorption spectral characteristics are taken into consideration, the ability of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst to degrade dexamethasone clearly depends on its ability to absorb light. Dexamethasone-degradation efficiencies of 66.1%, 59.0%, 72.0%, 52.6%, 38.4%, and 35.4% were recorded at 420, 460, 550, 578, 600, and 630 nm, respectively, with corresponding rate constants of 0.1098 h−1, 0.08420 h−1, 0.1798 h−1, 0.06000 h−1, 0.06927 h−1, and 0.02350 h−1, respectively. The CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower photocatalyst exhibited the highest photocurrent density at 550 nm (Fig. S14(c)), which is why it shows the highest photocatalytic activity at this wavelength.

Interestingly, approximately 68.2% of the dexamethasone was degraded by the TiO2 flower catalyst, which is equivalent to that of the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst, which raises obvious questions, namely: What is responsible for this? Is this observation related to adsorption? Fig. 5(a) shows that little dexamethasone was adsorbed by the TiO2 flowers in the dark after 30 min; however, this catalyst adsorbs slightly more than the other two photocatalysts. The adsorbed dexamethasone is likely to act as a self-sensitizer. In order to explain this observation in detail, we investigated the adsorption behavior of dexamethasone on the TiO2 flowers, which was followed by analysis of its degradation process by liquid chromatography and by determining the total organic carbon content (TOC) of the TiO2 flowers before and after dexamethasone degradation, the results of which are shown in Fig. S15. The TiO2 flowers required 1 h to adsorb dexamethasone to significantly higher levels at a temperature similar to that of the photocatalytic reaction (approximately 35 °C). Although circulating water was used to cool the system, the temperature of the photocatalytic system increased slightly with increasing irradiation light time (usually between 30 and 40 °C). Fig. S15(a) reveals that a higher temperature increases molecular thermal motion, which leads to a higher amount of dexamethasone being adsorbed on the surfaces of the TiO2 flowers. The degradation mixture was analyzed by HPLC (Fig. S15(b)), which showed that the TiO2 flowers mineralize dexamethasone in an unsatisfactory manner because dexamethasone and its degradation products are observed in the system after 4 h of irradiation, with 44.6% of the dexamethasone degraded according to TOC testing. Therefore, we believe that the dexamethasone degradation efficiency of 68.2% observed for the TiO2 flowers is mainly the result of adsorption. The TOC of the TiO2 flowers gradually increased from 0.38% to 1.6% (ΔTOC = 1.22%) as the dexamethasone degradation time was increased from 1 to 4 h (Fig. S15(c)); the TOC was lower after the solid catalyst was strongly sonicated, and the dexamethasone peak was not detected by liquid chromatography in the ultrasonicated solution (Fig. S15 (d)), which indicates that the TiO2 flower surfaces adsorb degradation products, and that the initially adsorbed dexamethasone may have been degraded. The TOC of the TiO2 flower surfaces gradually increased with increasing cycle number (Fig. S15(e)), which is possibly due to dexamethasone degradation products retained on the surface of the photocatalyst, which led to a significant decrease in degradation performance during the fourth and fifth cycles (from 68.2% to 29.7% and 27.7%, respectively) (Fig. 5(c)). However, the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst exhibited a dexamethasone mineralization degree of 46.4%, which is close to that determined for the pure TiO2 flowers; its solid ΔTOC is 0.03%, shown in (Table S3). After being compounded with the Cu-HQCA complex, the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers exhibited improved dexamethasone mineralization and degradation abilities, with pollutant removal mainly due to degradation rather than adsorption. The CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers exhibited a dexamethasone mineralization degree of 77.4%, which is higher than those of the pure TiO2 flowers and Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers; its solid ΔTOC was determined to be 0.02 %, as summarized in Table S3. These results show that the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst is better able to mineralize and degrade pollutants, with performance that far exceeds that of the TiO2 flower or Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst. The performance of a photocatalyst should not only be evaluated by focusing on target-compound reduction, but also on the degree of degradation and mineralization of the target compound. While the pristine TiO2 flowers and the other two modified TiO2 flowers showed almost identical degradation curves, their degradation and mineralization capabilities are significantly different.

As shown in Fig. S16(a), bacterial luminescent was significantly lower after the addition of 10 ppm dexamethasone, but was higher after photocatalytic degradation, with the highest intensity observed when the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower photocatalyst was used, which indicates that its degradation products are less environmentally toxic than those produced by the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers and TiO2 flowers, which was observed to depend on dexamethasone salinity produced by the three photocatalysts. A higher degree of salinity corresponds to more thorough dexamethasone degradation and lower environmental toxicity. Similarly, the lowest environmental toxicity was observed after dexamethasone was degraded using photocatalyst CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers at 550 nm (Fig. S16(b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation products and degradation process tracking of dexamethasone
The possible degradation sites of dexamethasone according to theoretical calculation
ce:section.ce:para: Determining the products formed by the degradation of dexamethasone is important for further evaluating the performance of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 photocatalyst. According to frontier orbital theory, atoms with the highest FED2 HOMO + FED2 LUMO (FED: frontier electron density) are most likely to be attacked by hydroxyl radicals and hydrogen peroxide. Atoms with high FED2 HOMO values may be attacked by holes, and those that are highly positively charged may be attacked by superoxide radical anions [69]. Similarly, carbon atoms with larger free valences are more easily attacked by free radicals, while bonds with lower bond orders are easier to break during photocatalysis [40]. FED2 HOMO, FED2 LUMO, free valence, and bond order values for the dexamethasone molecule were calculated at the B3LYP/6–31(d,p) level of theory, the results of which are summarized in Table 1 and shown in Fig. 7 .

The calculated C2-C6-F, C2-C3, C27-C28, C24-C28, C25-C24, C28-C34, C36-O39, and C28-O32 bond orders are relatively small, which indicates that these bonds are more easily broken during photocatalysis. C2, C54, and C36 were calculated to have the largest free valences, followed by C9, C13, C3, C50, C34, and C24, which suggests that these carbon atoms are likely to be attacked by free radicals. Furthermore, O19 has the highest FED2 HOMO value, followed by C12, C9, C7, C13, C8, and C6, which indicates that these atoms are easily attacked by photogenerated holes. Similarly, O19 has the largest 2FED2 HOMO + 2FED2 LUMO value, followed by C13, C12, C54, C25, and C7, which means that they are more easily attacked by hydroxyl radicals, while C2, C7, C11, C28, and C34 are the most positively charged, indicating that these positions can be attacked by superoxide radical. In summary, three possible sites dexamethasone are likely to be attacked during photocatalysis: the six-membered rings containing C6, C7, C9, C11, C12, C13, the five-membered ring, and the dihydroxyacetone side chain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation products and degradation process tracking of dexamethasone
ce:section.ce:para: The products formed by the degradation of dexamethasone over the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers for between 1 and 4 h were analyzed by liquid chromatography–high-resolution mass spectrometry, with m/z values, possible structures, and intensities listed in Table 2 , and the corresponding mass spectra shown in Fig. S17.

P1 and P11 were the main fragments observed after the first hour of degradation according to the observed signal intensities, while P3 and P4 were the main fragments after 2 h, P5 and P13 after 3 h, and P8, P9, P10 and P17 after 4 h. Clearly, most fragments are small ring-opened molecules after 4 h of degradation.

Three possible dexamethasone degradation pathways are proposed based on the intensities of the fragment signals and corresponding structures (Fig. 8 ). In pathway I, •O2 − first attacks the C2 position to form defluorinated product P1. The C12 and C13 positions of P1 are further attacked by photogenerated holes and •OH to form P2 and P3, with C13, C12, and O19 also continuously attacked by •OH, C7 attacked by photogenerated holes, and C2 attacked by •O2 −, resulting in C9-C12-C11, C9-C7-C8, and C1-C2-C6 cleavage to form P4, P5, and P6, with P7, P8, P9, and P10 the products resulting from C25, C28, and C2, respectively. Finally, these fragments are further mineralized into CO2 and H2O. Hydroxylated product P11 is obtained by •OH attack at the C9 position of dexamethasone, with P12 formed after dehydration, as shown in pathway II. P12 is then oxidized to P13 by •OH and •O2 −, which then finally decomposes into CO2 and H2O. Products P14 and P15 are formed in pathway III when •O2 − first attack positions C28 and C34. The five-membered ring contains carbon atoms with low bonding levels, while the dihydroxyacetone side chain contains carbon atoms that have large free valences; hence, these structures are easily cleaved during photocatalysis; hence, degradation products P15, P16, P17, and P18 are produced. The above discussion reveals that the main dexamethasone structure is destroyed with mainly small ring-opened fragments formed as the final degradation products, which explains the significant reduction in the environmental toxicity of dexamethasone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of dexamethasone degradation by CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower
ce:section.ce:para: In order to accurately elucidate the mechanistic details of the excellent photocatalytic performance of the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst, we also investigated the efficiency of the Cu-HQCA@CDs/TiO2 flower catalyst, in which the TiO2 flowers are first reacted with CDs and then with the Cu-HQCA complex, to degrade dexamethasone, the results of which are shown in Fig. S18 together with those of the CDs/TiO2 flower and Cu-HQAC/TiO2 flower reference catalysts. The CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst clearly exhibits the highest dexamethasone degradation efficiency, followed by the Cu-HQAC/TiO2 flowers and the CDs/TiO2 flowers, with the Cu-HQCA@CDs/TiO2 flower catalyst showing the lowest degradation efficiency.

The degradation efficiencies of the four photocatalysts were examined in the presence of the same amounts of various free-radical traps, as shown in Fig. S19. Isopropyl alcohol, p-benzoquinone, and EDTA-2Na were used to trap •OH, •O2 −, and photogenerated holes (h+), respectively. The binary Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower photocatalyst mainly produced •O2 − and •OH, while the CDs/TiO2 flowers produced mainly h+ and •O2 −. On the other hand, the ternary CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower photocatalyst mainly generated h+, •O2 −, and •OH, while the Cu-HQCA@CDs/TiO2 flower catalyst generated only h+. Clearly, the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst generated the highest number of active species. Holes directly oxidize dexamethasone and play major roles in photocatalytic reaction systems containing CDs. The ESR data (Fig. S20) show that the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flowers produce significantly more •O2 − and •OH (1.5 × 106, 5 × 104) than the CDs/TiO2 flowers (1 × 104, 2 × 104). On the other hand, CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst generated far more •O2 − and •OH (2 × 106, 6 × 105) than the Cu-HQCA@CDs/TiO2 flowers (1 × 104, 5 × 103); the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 catalyst produced more of each radical when irradiated with visible light.

The XPS and UV–vis spectra (Fig. S21) of the abovementioned four catalysts were used to determine the TiO2 bandgap and the VB CB, HOMO, and LUMO energies of the Cu-HQAC complexes and CDs in each system, with possible photogenerated carrier migration pathways presented in Fig. 9 . The VB and the CB energies of TiO2 were determined to be 2.90 eV and − 0.20 eV, respectively, with HOMO and LUMO energies of 1.63 eV and 0.44 eV, and − 0.78 eV and − 1.44 eV determined for the Cu-HQCA complexes and CDs, respectively.

The work functions of the TiO2 flower, Cu-HQCA complexes, and CDs determined by UV photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS; Fig. S22) are 4.82 eV, 4.72 eV, and 5.62 eV, respectively, which means that electrons easily transfer from the Cu-HQCA complexes to the TiO2 or CDs, but transferal in the opposite direction is difficult. Based on the bandgap and the types and strengths of the active moieties, we propose four possible mechanisms for the photocatalytic degradation of dexamethasone.

Electrons in the LUMO of the Cu-HQCA complexes migrate to the CB of TiO2 and interact with adsorbed oxygen to generate •O2 −. At the same time, electrons in the LUMO of the Cu-HQCA complex directly interact with oxygen to generate •OH. Consequently, the probability of electrons recombining with holes in the HOMO is reduced and the holes directly oxidize dexamethasone.

In this system, the TiO2 flower is only the CD matrix; hence no electrons are transferred between the TiO2 flower and the CDs. Photogenerated electrons and holes recombine very easily owing to the narrow CD bandgap, which reduces the number of •O2 − and holes and impacts photocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, the CDs/TiO2 flower photocatalyst performs more poorly than the Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst.

Multiple electron transfer pathways between the TiO2 flowers, Cu-HQCA complexes, and CDs exist for the ternary CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower photocatalyst. First, electrons transfer from the LUMO of the Cu-HQCA complex to the CB of the TiO2 flower and reacts with the oxygen adsorbed on the surface of the TiO2 to generate •O2 −. Second, electrons in the HOMO are easily excited to the LUMO owing to the small CD bandgap. At the same time, electrons in the LUMO of Cu-HQCA migrate to the HOMO of the CDs and recombine with holes. This accelerates electron/hole separation efficiency in Cu-HQCA, which is beneficial for accumulating holes in the HOMO; these holes then directly decompose dexamethasone. Finally, electron transport between the CDs and the complexes eventually leads to a higher number of electrons in the CDs LUMO, thereby generating more •O2 − and •OH, and more holes in the HOMO of the complex. In addition, electron transfer between the complex and TiO2 effectively weakens photogenerated electron/hole recombination in the complex. The abovementioned pathways act in synergy to engender the CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst with excellent photocatalytic activity.

The TiO2 flowers are covered by CDs and Cu-HQCA complexes that are directly bound to the CDs, thereby blocking electron transfer between the complex and the TiO2 flower, which increases the probability that the electrons in the LUMO of the complex return to the HOMO to recombine with holes. At the same time, the probability that electrons in the LUMOs of the Cu-HQCA complexes recombine with holes in the HOMOs of the CDs decreases, which increases electron/hole recombination in the CDs; consequently, few electrons and holes participate in the photocatalytic reaction, which explains why the Cu-HQCA@CDs/TiO2 flower photocatalyst performs poorly.

Four identically composed binary Cu-HQCA/TiO2 photocatalysts with different morphologies are universally able to photocatalytically degrade organic and inorganic pollutants. The CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 flower catalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance, decomposing 77.4% of the dexamethasone in a short time. On the one hand, activity depends on the morphology of the TiO2 flower, which is conducive to adsorbing pollutants and improving photoelectric response characteristics. On the other hand, activity is also derived from the excellent electron transport abilities of the CDs. The Cu-HQCA complex successfully expanded the visible-light absorption characteristics of the composite photocatalyst. The results of this study demonstrate that designing and constructing a highly efficient composite photocatalyst is feasible through synergy between complex sensitization, TiO2 morphology control, and CDs surface modification.
